EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance and its associated cold front moved into West Virginia on the 25th. Hot and humid conditions ahead of the front led to moderate amounts of instability. Showers and thunderstorms developed and some thunderstorms became severe due to the instability and strong winds aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down on route 50 near the Potomac River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant rain advanced north into the region in advance of an area of low pressure to the west while a frontal boundary remained draped over the region. An average of 2 to 5 inches of rain fell from this system onto an already saturated ground from recent snow melt and rainfall. The heavy rain contributed to widespread flash flooding, mudslides, areal flooding, and river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain prompted flash flooding of a stream across Tuckahoe Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front move through southeast West Virginia the morning of January 8, 2010. Behind this front, increasing northwest winds with Great Lake moisture helped to generate up to ten inches of snow in the western slopes of Greenbrier County West Virginia. The bulk of the snow fell through the morning hours on January 9, but additional light accumulating snow continue through the early afternoon of January 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The snow storm produced ten inches of snow at Quinwood, West Virginia.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretions totaled 0.25 inches in both Frankford and Ronceverte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure strengthened over the central portion of the nation on the fifth of February. The storm system slowly moved through the Mid-Atlantic during the night of the 5th before redeveloping off the Mid-Atlantic coast on 6th. The storm system finally moved away from the area on the night of the 6th.||Strong high pressure continued to pump in plenty of cold air across the region for the entire event. Due to the slow movement of the storm, there was a prolonged period of precipitation. The storm system ushered in copious amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The deep moisture combined with the forcing from the storm system to bring a period of heavy precipitation to the area on the night of the 5th through the daytime hours on the 6th. Precipitation finally ended during the evening hours of the 6th as the storm system moved away. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of snow due to the cold air that was already in place.||Major snow accumulations were reported throughout the state of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 29.5 inches near Smith Crossroads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved east across the upper Ohio Valley bringing mixed precipitation followed by cold northwest flow. Lake effect snow bands produced heavy snow in Mercer county Pennsylvania, as well as the ridges in Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. Heavy snow also fell in the ridges of Preston and Tucker counties of West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Storm totals of 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across portions of pennsylavania, with more than one foot of snow in portions of West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland.

Cold northwest winds behind strong low pressure moving off the east coast produced heavy lake effect snow across the higher elevations of Preston and Tucker counties with a storm total of 8 to 16 inches.

Lake effect snow bands produced heavy snow in Mercer county Pennsylvania, as well as the ridges in Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. Heavy snow also fell in the ridges of Preston and Tucker counties of West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Storm totals of 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across portions of pennsylavania, with more than one foot of snow in portions of West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland.

Only 3 days after an historic snow storm hit the region, another major storm blasted portions of western Pennyslvania, northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and eastern Ohio from the afternoon of February 9th to the early morning of February 11th. Blizzard conditions occurred in Garrett county Maryland, Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, and in the higher elevations of Fayette and Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania. Over 2 feet of snow fell across Garrett, Preston and Tucker counties with 9 to 24 inches of snow in Fayette and Westmoreland counties. Snowfall amounts ranged from 28 inches at Red House, MD to 26 inches at Davis, WV, to 19 inches in Farmington, PA. Winds gusting over 35 MPH in the higher elevations caused whiteout conditions at times with visibilities below one quarter mile for an extended period. Elsewhere in western Pennsylvania snowfall was from 8 to 12 inches generally in and north of Pittsburgh to north of I-80. An isolated area of heavier snow also fell in and around New Philadelphia in Tuscarawas county OH, with 8 inches of snow in New Cumberland, OH. In the remainder of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia 3 to 5 inches of snow fell.

A potent area of low pressure tracked through the Midwest on the 9th. As the low approached the Mid-Atlantic coast, this system phased with energy in the southern branch of the jet stream to cause strong low pressure to develop just off the Delmarva peninsula. The low continued to rapidly strengthen as it moved off to our northeast on the 10th. ||The system spread snow across West Virginia that began on the 9th and lasted into the 10th. Significant snow accumulations were reported with this storm. As the low pressure rapidly intensified on the 10th, strong winds caused blowing and drifting snow that led to blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be over 35 mph along with visibility below one quarter mile to produce blizzard conditions.

A large and slow moving area of low pressure developed across the Ohio valley on the 25th and intensified as it moved east to the Mid-Atlantic states. Heavy snow fell in the ridges of Garrett county Maryland, northern West Virginia, and western Pennsylvania with storm totals of 12 to 36 inches. Highest reported storm totals were 34 inches at Red House in Garrett county, MD and 33 inches in Farmington in Fayette county, PA. Northwest winds gusting at times over 30 MPH also caused severe blowing and drifting of snow with drifts of 8 to 10 feet reported. Elsewhere over western Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia a prolonged snow event of 36 to 48 hours brought storm totals of 6 to 10 inches with minor blowing and drufting of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Atlantic Ocean continued to pump in hot and humid air over the Mid-Atlantic. A series of upper-level disturbances in the jet stream passed through eastern West Virginia triggering showers and thunderstorms during this time. There was enough instability from the hot and humid air for some thunderstorms to contain damaging winds during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were down down near Elk Garden.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace of ice was reported at Keyser.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved through New England on the 14th while Canadian high pressure approached from the Great Lakes. The pressure gradient between these two systems brought windy conditions to the Potomac Highlands of West Virginia. The gusty winds combined with cold temperatures to produce dangerously low wind chill values during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values were estimated to be around -20F across western Pendleton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure remained over the region from the fifth of December through the 8th. A northwest flow around the trough continued to pump in plenty of cold air. The cold air picked up moisture from the Great Lakes and deposited it in the form of snow for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front. Gusty winds and low visibility produced blizzard conditions on the 6th of December. The heaviest snow fell during the fifth and sixth of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were reported by a trained spotter near Circleville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trace of ice was reported at Elk Garden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure remained over the region from the fifth of December through the 8th. A northwest flow around the trough continued to pump in plenty of cold air. The cold air picked up moisture from the Great Lakes and deposited it in the form of snow for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front. Gusty winds and low visibility produced blizzard conditions on the 6th of December. The heaviest snow fell during the fifth and sixth of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were reported by a trained spotter in western Mineral County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed through the region in a zonal flow causing precipitation to develop on the 16th. There was plenty of cold air in place ahead of this system causing precipitation to fall in the form of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 2.2 inches near Springfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure rapidly intensified on the 26th as it moved up the Mid-Atlantic coast. A northwest flow behind this system transported plenty of cold air across the Potomac Highlands of West Virginia. The cold air combined with moisture from the Great Lakes to trigger snow showers for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 7.4 inches at Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Atlantic Ocean continued to pump in hot and humid air over the Mid-Atlantic. A series of upper-level disturbances in the jet stream passed through eastern West Virginia triggering showers and thunderstorms during this time. There was enough instability from the hot and humid air for some thunderstorms to contain damaging winds during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure moved up the Mid-Atlantic coast producing widespread heavy rainfall. The 2 to 4 inches of rain combined with nearly saturated antecedent conditions to produce flooding over eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several Roads across the county were closed due to high water, including John Wrissler Road, Bloomery Road, and River Road. Weather observer in the area recorded 2.63 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low passed through the Mid-Atlantic on the night of the 24th. The upper-level low combined with energy in the southern branch of the jet stream, causing low pressure to rapidly intensify over New England on the 25th into the 26th. Low pressure remained nearly stationary before finally weakening and moving off to the east on the 28th.||A northwest flow around the low triggered a prolonged period of upslope snow for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front. Gusty winds and locally heavy snow reduced visibility to near zero at times, especially on the night of the 25th into the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 45.7 inches in Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Ohio and the northern panhandle of West Virginia during the afternoon along a warm front moving north ahead of low pressure. As the surface low pushed east a cold front brought additional showers and thunderstorms that produced flash flooding in eastern Ohio in the evening.

Severe thunderstorms were scattered across eastern Ohio, the northern West Virginia panhandle, and southwest Pennsylvania ahead of a cold front. Significant damaging winds associated with a large bow echo occured along a path from near Steubenville, Ohio to Latrobe, Pennsylvania. From this bow echo the highest recorded wind gusts at ASOS observation sites were 71 MPH at Wheeling, WV, 66 MPH at Allegheny County Airport in West Mifflin, PA, and 86 MPH at Latrobe, PA. At least 150,000 homes lost power at some point during the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and law enforcement reported trees and power lines down in and around Weirton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure tracked through the Midwest on the 9th. As the low approached the Mid-Atlantic coast, this system phased with energy in the southern branch of the jet stream to cause strong low pressure to develop just off the Delmarva peninsula. The low continued to rapidly strengthen as it moved off to our northeast on the 10th. ||The system spread snow across West Virginia that began on the 9th and lasted into the 10th. Significant snow accumulations were reported with this storm. As the low pressure rapidly intensified on the 10th, strong winds caused blowing and drifting snow that led to blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 11.4 inches in Berkeley Springs. Snowfall also totaled up to 7.0 inches at Smith Crossroads.

A stationary boundary remained over eastern West Virginia on the 27th. A northwest flow aloft provided cold air advection at the mid-levels of the atmosphere. The combination of warm and humid conditions near the surface and colder air aloft led to moderate amounts of instability. Some of the thunderstorms that developed near the front became severe. Large hail was the primary threat due to the colder air aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported near Brandywine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary remained over eastern West Virginia on the 28th. An easterly flow off the Atlantic Ocean pumped in plenty of moisture. Showers and thunderstorms developed near the boundary. Due to a light steering flow aloft showers and thunderstorms were slow to move. Therefore, heavy amounts of rain fell in short periods of time across eastern West Virginia. This resulted in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A medical building was flooded. There were mudslides and flooded roads reported throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of low pressure areas moved across the Ohio valley with cold northwest developing behind it. Bands of heavier snow fell across Mercer and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania, Garrett county Maryland, and the ridges of northern West Virginia. Snowfall totals ranged from 8 to 14 inches in these areas, with lesser amounts across the remainder of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.

Low pressure moving east across the Ohio valley produced a mix of precipitation with freezing rain and sleet. Ice accumulations in Garrett county Maryland and Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia ranged between a quarter and one half inch.

Low pressure moving across the Ohio valley brought rain and warmer temperatures to the region. This combination led to snow melt and widespread flooding along the Cheat, Youghiogheny, and Monongalhela rivers, with the Ohio river at Pittsburgh also rising above flood stage. Minor flooding also ocurred along Shavers Fork in Tucker County West Virginia from Porterwood to near Parsons. Water topped the flood wall near Parsons the afternoon of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flooding along Shavers Fork from Porterwood to near Parsons. Shavers Fork topped the flood wall for a time in Parsons.

Moist northwest flow behind a powerful cold front produced snow showers with heavy snow accumulations across Garrett county Maryland, and the ridges of northern West Virginia. Storm totals ranged from 8 to 12 inches across this region. Lighter snow fell across portions of northwest Pennsylvania but accumulations were light.

Multiple, slow moving, heavy rain producing thunderstorms progressed across the same regions over a short period of time. The large volume of water in narrow creek basins prompted significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains prompted flash flooding in Rainelle.  A total of 10 to 15 people needed to be evacuated from their homes. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad low pressure swept cold moist air across the upper Ohio valley. Heavy snow fell across Garrett county Maryland as well as the ridges of northern West Virginia and Fayette county in Pennsylvania. Snow showers accumulated 8 to 16 inches across these regions with the larger amounts across higher elevations. Snow showers across the remiander of western Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia produced light accumulations.

Another in a series of low pressure areas moved across the Ohio valley with cold northwest developing behind it. Bands of heavier snow fell across Mercer and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania, Garrett county Maryland, and the ridges of northern West Virginia. Snowfall totals ranged from 8 to 14 inches in these areas, with lesser amounts across the remainder of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.

A strong cold front passed through the region late during the evening of the 16th into the early morning morning hours of the 17th. A strong southerly flow ahead of the cold front transported plenty of moisture into the area allowing for limited instability to develop. Strong forcing associated with the frontal passage combined with limited instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Thunderstorms were able to mix down very strong winds aloft causing damaging winds across some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 70 mph was measured near Brooke Haven.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level low slowly tracked through the central portion of the country on the 25th. A southwest flow ahead of this system ushered in warm and humid air. A cold front dropped into the region from the northeast during the afternoon and evening hours of the 25th. The cold front combined with plenty of instability for showers and thunderstorms to develop. Due to plenty of cold air aloft, thunderstorms produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was near Middleway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell early on Christmas morning.  A uniform 1 to 2 inches of snow accumulated.  After a lull Christmas afternoon, snow showers increased Christmas night.  The snow showers became most widespread during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th.  This increase was in response to a developing coastal storm and its associated mid level support.  The snow showers decreased by the morning hours of the 27th.   An additional accumulation of 4 to 6 inches of fluffy dry snow was common over about a 36 hour period.  However, in the usual maximum upslope areas of Randolph, Webster, Nicholas, Upshur, and western Pocahontas Counties, the 36 hour snow accumulation was 6 to 12 inches.

As a cold front moved southeast across the upper Ohio Valley, scattered severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. A supercell thunderstorm produced an EF1 tornado in Carroll and Jefferson counties in Ohio, with numerous reports of wind damage and hail up to golf ball size elsewhere. Flooding of streams and creeks also occurred over western Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and the northern panhandle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported White Road in Newell flooded due to heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved along a boundary situated over east central Ohio, southern Pennsylvania and Northern West Virginia. Severe thunderstorms across eastern Ohio, southwest Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland produced widespread wind damage with 50 thousand homes left without power. The counties that were hardest hit were Belmont and Jefferson counties in Ohio, where numerous large trees blocked roadways and three large radio station towers collapsed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported numerous trees and power lines down in Wellsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a strong cold front, much colder air blew in during Sunday the 12th.  Rain changed to snow early that morning.  Banded upslope snow showers persisted into Monday evening the 13th, then diminished overnight.  Temperatures were in the teens to around 20 degrees on the 13th. ||Accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were widespread across McDowell County.

In the wake of a clipper system, a prolong upslope snow shower event started during Sunday afternoon the 5th.  The snow showers reached their peak coverage and intensity on Monday the 6th, then diminished during Tuesday afternoon the 7th.   Snow depths prior to the storm were mostly 2 to 5 inches.  A snowfall of 8 to 16 inches was common in about a 48 hour period.  The lower limit was measured across much of Fayette County, while the upper limit was observed across the high terrain of Nicholas, Upshur, Randolph, Pocahontas, and Webster Counties.  Some point snowfalls included Snowshoe with 16 inches, Pickens 17 inches, Richwood and Hacker Valley around 16 inches, Rock Cave 14 inches, Webster Springs and Valley Head 13 inches, and finally Oak Hill with 8 inches.  The downslope areas into the Greenbrier River Valley of Pocahontas County had less snow. ||Total snow depths reached to around 20 inches near Richwood and Pickens before settling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Appalachian Mountains early on the morning of December 1 into an unseasonably warm and moisture atmosphere in the Mid-Atlantic. Widespread rain and thunderstorms developed, some of which produced heavy rain and flash flooding across the area. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to over 2 inches across most locations, with the highest report received in Berkeley County of 2.57 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water in association with New Creek was reported on Route 46. Rainfall in the area measured 1.79 inches.

In the wake of a clipper system, a prolong upslope snow shower event started during Sunday afternoon the 5th.  The snow showers reached their peak coverage and intensity on Monday the 6th, then diminished during Tuesday afternoon the 7th.   Snow depths prior to the storm were mostly 2 to 5 inches.  A snowfall of 8 to 16 inches was common in about a 48 hour period.  The lower limit was measured across much of Fayette County, while the upper limit was observed across the high terrain of Nicholas, Upshur, Randolph, Pocahontas, and Webster Counties.  Some point snowfalls included Snowshoe with 16 inches, Pickens 17 inches, Richwood and Hacker Valley around 16 inches, Rock Cave 14 inches, Webster Springs and Valley Head 13 inches, and finally Oak Hill with 8 inches.  The downslope areas into the Greenbrier River Valley of Pocahontas County had less snow. ||Total snow depths reached to around 20 inches near Richwood and Pickens before settling.

A broken squall line ahead of a cold front moved across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia during the afternoon producing multiple reports of straight line wind damge. At one point more than 50 thousand homes were without power due to power lines being down across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a large tree blocking Rolling Acres RD in New Cumberland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong northwest winds developed in the wake of a departing cold front. The persistent trajectories and duration of the event helped snow accumulate up to over one foot at the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts across the county ranged from 6.0 inches at Bramwell to 10.0 inches near Wenonah. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level cold air came south and southwest into the upper Greenbrier River Valley from northern Virginia during the morning of the 21st.  Freezing rain, with temperatures in the upper 20s, fell across Pocahontas County.  Ice accumulation was a quarter of an inch in numerous places throughout the county.
Very strong northwest winds developed in the wake of a departing cold front. The persistent trajectories and duration of the event helped snow accumulate up to over one foot at the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts across the county ranged from 6.0 inches at Jumping Branch to 7.0 inches at Ellison Ridge. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through eastern West Virginia triggering snow showers. The heaviest snow fell across locations along and west of the Allegheny front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was estimated to be over five inches across the western part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through eastern West Virginia triggering snow showers. The heaviest snow fell across locations along and west of the Allegheny front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was reported around 10.5 inches in Bayard and around 5.0 inches in Mount Storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through eastern West Virginia. The combination of cold moist air moving over the mountains triggered a prolonged period of snow showers for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snow accumulation from the 3rd through the 6th of January at Bayard was 7.2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of rain began early on the 24th and became heavier overnight into the 25th.  Dew points rose into the upper 40s to around 50s.  The rain diminished to a few lingering showers with a cold front during the day on the 25th.  Rain amounts of 1 to 1.5 inches were common.  However, amounts near 3 inches were measured around Woodrow, Mace and Black Mountain.  Snowshoe reported 3.9 inches for the maximum amount.  This rain and snow melt combined to cause minor small stream flooding across Nicholas, Webster, Pocahontas, and Randolph Counties.  Minor river flooding did occur along the upper Greenbrier River in Pocahontas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams feeding into the Shavers Fork and Glady Fork flooded roads and surrounded recreational cabins.  The Elkins Fire Department rescued 4 people and their pets.  Using ropes, they waded threw waist deep water, to reach a cabin in Bowden around dawn.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals were estimated to be over five inches across the western part of Mineral County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled to 6.0 inches in Mount Storm and 6.3 inches in Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frequent snow showers in a colder northwest upslope flow resulted in a 5 to 10 inch snowfall.  The dry and fluffy nature of the snow, along with sustained winds of 10 to 20 mph, resulted in blowing snow.  Temperatures across the high ridges dropped to near zero during the 2nd.  Those same northwest winds descended into the Greenbrier Valley in Pocahontas County, resulting in less snow there.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first small stream flooding was reported around Slatyfork, followed by flooding along Knapp Creek.  The restaurant along Route 66 in Cass had water up to its foundation.  The Greenbrier River crested at Marlinton around 13.7 feet and at Buckeye near 16.8 feet. Flood stage is 15 feet at the forecast point at Buckeye.  About 35 homes were affected in Marlinton, mainly on the south end of town near the mouth of Knapp Creek.  However, most of the downtown business area escaped without getting water inside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure rapidly intensified on the 26th as it moved up the Mid-Atlantic coast. A northwest flow behind this system transported plenty of cold air across the Potomac Highlands of West Virginia. The cold air combined with moisture from the Great Lakes to trigger snow showers for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals around six inches were estimated across western Pendleton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure remained over the region from the fifth of December through the 8th. A northwest flow around the trough continued to pump in plenty of cold air. The cold air picked up moisture from the Great Lakes and deposited it in the form of snow for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front. Gusty winds and low visibility produced blizzard conditions on the 6th of December. The heaviest snow fell during the fifth and sixth of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were reported by a Coop Observer in Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure sitting over the eastern Great Lakes brought prolonged cold northwest flow across the region producing intense lake effect snow bands into northwest Pennsylvania and the higher ridges of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Storm total snowfall ranged from 6 to 10 inches with isolated 12 inch reports across northwest Pennsylvania and extended south into Butler county. Amounts in the ridges of western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland ranged from 12 to 18 inches.

A major storm affected the eastern U.S. during the period from November 30-December 1 as a powerful upper low and surface system moved into the Great Lakes providing an extended period of deep southerly flow across the region. Precipitable water values were extremely high for very early December, running from 1.3 to 1.6 inches or nearly 200 percent of normal.  Precipitation developed late on the 29th and produced moderate rains (0.25 to 0.50���) across the area for the 24 hours ending 12Z on the 30th. Heavier and more widespread precipitation developed on the evening of the 30th into the early morning hours of the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brush Creek flooded Stafford Drive for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system developed from the central Appalachians south to the Gulf of Mexico. The first of two areas of low pressure brought relatively light amounts of snow to all but the eastern Piedmont region of Virginia and North Carolina. The second low took a track that took it through central North Carolina. Snow on the western flanks of this low brought a greater amount of snow to the region. Snowfall amounts varied significantly during this event, and were elevation dependent. Amounts ranged from as little as 2.0 inches to as much as 10.5 inches over southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of eight inches of snow was reported near Sandstone. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With low pressure well to the south, and temperatures in the teens and lower 20s, a dry snow gradually moved north, into extreme southern West Virginia late on Friday the 29th.  The snow creeped further north during the morning hours of Saturday the 30th.  The heaviest snow rates were before dawn in the southern coal field counties, then later that morning further to the north. The northern fringe of the heavy snow was near the Kanawha Valley around Charleston on east toward Summersville of Nicholas County.  Much lighter snow reached as far north as Parkersburg and Clarksburg during the day on the 30th. ||Snow accumulations of 3 to 5 inches fell in 12 hours across Wayne, Lincoln, Boone, and Kanawha Counties.  Snow accumulations of 5 to 6 inches fell in 12 hours across Clay and Nicholas Counties.  Further south, snow accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were common across Mingo, Logan, Wyoming, McDowell, Raleigh, and Fayette Counties.  Some of the higher terrain in McDowell County reported snow accumulations near a foot.

The preferred upslope region of western Greenbrier County received around 15 inches of snow during the two day event. Combined with strong winds gusting to 30 mph, drifting and blow snow were also reported. Wind chill values dipped into the zero degrees to ten below zero range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total amounts ranged from 10 inches at Rainelle to 15 inches at Quinwood.

Broad low pressure swept cold moist air across the upper Ohio valley. Heavy snow fell across Garrett county Maryland as well as the ridges of northern West Virginia and Fayette county in Pennsylvania. Snow showers accumulated 8 to 16 inches across these regions with the larger amounts across higher elevations. Snow showers across the remiander of western Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia produced light accumulations.

Low pressure moving east across the Ohio valley produced a mix of precipitation with freezing rain and sleet. Ice accumulations in Garrett county Maryland and Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia ranged between a quarter and one half inch.

The first of 2 prolonged snow events during the latter half of February, began on the 15th.  It started as a quick shot of snow preceding a low pressure system moving northeast, along the Ohio River.  Accumulations were mostly 2 inches or less.  ||The wrap around snow increased after 0000E on Tuesday the 16th.  By dawn on the 16th, 5 to 10 inches of new snow had fallen across the high terrain, with 4 to 6 inches in the valleys across the mountainous counties. ||The upslope snow showers increased in coverage and intensity late on the 16th, as an upper air disturbance swung through.   The fluffy and wind blown snow showers continued on Wednesday the 17th, before finally decreasing early on Thursday the 18th.  ||A snowfall around a foot was common across the lower elevations, while 2 feet fell in the high elevations.  For example, Rock Cave, Buckhannon, Belington, and Hacker Valley all measured a snowfall around 12 inches.  Glady had 16 inches of snowfall.  Valley Head had 22 inches, Snowshoe 24 inches, and Pickens 25 inches.  ||The total snow pack was now 1 to 2 feet along the river valleys in these mountainous counties, with 3 feet common across high terrain.  This was not considering drifts. The total snow depth at Snowshoe peaked at 43 inches.

For example, Rock Cave, Buckhannon, Belington, and Hacker Valley all measured a snowfall around 12 inches.  Glady had 16 inches of snowfall.  Valley Head had 22 inches, Snowshoe 24 inches, and Pickens 25 inches.  ||The total snow pack was now 1 to 2 feet along the river valleys in these mountainous counties, with 3 feet common across high terrain. A wintry mix of precipitation arrived during the morning of Tuesday the 9th.  However, the mixture changed to mostly rain for the afternoon, as the low pressure center lifted northeast toward Ohio.  The artcic cold front swept through around 0000E on Wednesday the 10th.  The rain had changed to snow Tuesday evening, prior to the frontal passage.  ||As the coastal storm strengthened during the daylight hours of Wednesday the 10th, wind blown snow showers were seen over West Virginia.  Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph were felt.  The snow showers diminished that evening over the lowlands.  However, the snow showers did not diminish until early on the 11th for the mountainous counties. ||The lowlands saw widespread accumulations of 2 to 5 inches over 24 hours.  The heavy snow was limited to the mountainous counties.  Snow accumulations of 6 to 12 inches were common. The extreme case was at Kumbrabow State Forest, where the snow depth increased from 15 inches prior to the storm, to 29 inches.  Pickens snow depth went from 6 inches to 15 inches.  Glady snow depth increased from 12 inches prior to this storm, to 19 inches.   Beckley saw their snow depth go from 7 inches before the storm, to 17 inches.  On a mountain near Richwood, their snow depth went from 8 inches before the storm, to 19 inches by dawn on the 11th.  Elkins new snow accumulation was 6 inches, with a total snow depth of 13 inches at the end of this storm.    In the river valley at Webster Springs, a 6 inch snow accumulation was also measured.

The 2nd prolonged period of snow showers in the later half of February occurred from late on Wednesday the 24th into early on Sunday the 28th.||The event started as a strong upper air disturbance crossed the area late on the 24th into Thursday the 25th.  Winds increased late on the 25th into midday Friday the 26th, as the surface storm intensified along the coast.  The snow showers continued on the 26th.  Blowing snow and frequent wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph caused near blizzard conditions across the open high terrain of Randolph, Webster, Nicholas, and Pocahontas Counties.  A peak gust of 47 mph was noted on the mountaintop at Snowshoe.  Route 32 and 33 were briefly closed down in northeastern Randolph County by the poor travel conditions.  Winds subsided by late afternoon on the 26th.||Late on Friday the 26th into Saturday the 27th, deeper moisture wrapped south, around the nearly stationary storm that was in the vicinity of New York City.   The lowland counties from Pleasants, Tyler, Doddridge, and Harrison, on south to Kanawha and Boone saw their peak in the snow showers at this time.  Snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches occurred there, in about a 12 hour period.||The storm slowly weakened on Saturday the 27th.    ||Over the high mountains a total snowfall of 2 to 3 feet occurred over a 3 day period.  One to 2 feet of snow fell along many of the river valleys in the mountainous counties over the same period.  Of course, due to settling of the deep snow pack, the increase in the snow depth was less than the snowfall.   Pickens reported a 38 inch snowfall.  Their snow depth went from 12 inches prior to the storm, to 30 inches.  The mountain observer near Richwood had a 30 inch snowfall, with their snow depth increasing from 8 inches to 32 inches.  Valley Head had around 26 inches of snow fall with their snow depth increasing from 7 inches to 24 inches.  Glady had a 24 inch snowfall.  The snow depth at Glady was 12 inches prior to the storm.  The snow depth at Glady was 28 inches at its conclusion.  Buckhannon had a 23 inches of snow fall.  Their snow depth went from 4 inches to 23 inches.  Elkins had a 16 inch snow fall.  The snow depth at Elkins went from 7 inches prior to the storm, to 17 inches at the conclusion.  Beckley also had over a foot of snowfall with their snow depth increasing from 2 inches to 14 inches. ||The month ended with over 100 inches of snowfall at Snowshoe.  Several other mountain locations had around 75 inches or more of snowfall in February.  The monthly snowfall totals of 45 inches at Beckley, 44 inches at Elkins, 38 inches at Clarksburg, and nearly 26 inches at Charleston were the most on record for the month of February at those sites.

A continuous rain on Friday the 5th became heavier in the evening over the southwestern counties of West Virginia.  Twelve hour rains of over an inch caused minor stream flooding along roads across portions of Lincoln, Putnam, Cabell, Kanawha, Roane, Jackson, Mason, and Calhoun.   The rain changed to sleet then wet snow overnight, or late on the 5th into the 6th.    ||An exception was Pocahontas County, where the easterly low level flow kept the precipitation as mostly snow.  At Snowshoe the snow depth went from 10 inches prior to the storm to 27 inches at the conclusion. At Marlinton the snow depth went from 7 inches to 20 inches.  At Bartow the snow depth went from 5 inches to 13 inches.  ||The other exception was across Fayette and Raleigh Counties in the southern mountains of West Virginia.  Here, pockets of freezing rain fell during the daylight hours on the 5th, before changing to snow that night.  The ice from the freezing rain accumulated to around a quarter of an inch.  The snow accumulations were mostly |2 to 4 inches.||The heavy wet snow caused power outages across the northern lowland counties of West Virginia.  On the 11th, several days after the storm, Harrison County still had hundreds of structures without power.   Clarksburg snow depth went from zero prior to the storm to 11 inches.   Grafton had a 9 inch accumulation.  Harrisville had an 8 inch snow accumulation, while Middlebourne measured 7 inches.  Elkins had a 6 inch snow accumulation.||Further south, toward Glenville and Sutton, snow accumulations of 5 to 6 inches were more common.||In the central mountain counties of Nicholas and Webster, not enough freezing rain or snow fell to verify the winter storm warning||Since the storm had been predicted well in advance, Governor Joe Manchin declared a state of emergency at the very onset of the storm, on the 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of precipitation arrived during the morning of Tuesday the 9th.  However, the mixture changed to mostly rain for the afternoon, as the low pressure center lifted northeast toward Ohio.  The artcic cold front swept through around 0000E on Wednesday the 10th.  The rain had changed to snow Tuesday evening, prior to the frontal passage.  ||As the coastal storm strengthened during the daylight hours of Wednesday the 10th, wind blown snow showers were seen over West Virginia.  Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph were felt.  The snow showers diminished that evening over the lowlands.  However, the snow showers did not diminish until early on the 11th for the mountainous counties. ||The lowlands saw widespread accumulations of 2 to 5 inches over 24 hours.  The heavy snow was limited to the mountainous counties.  Snow accumulations of 6 to 12 inches were common. The extreme case was at Kumbrabow State Forest, where the snow depth increased from 15 inches prior to the storm, to 29 inches.  Pickens snow depth went from 6 inches to 15 inches.  Glady snow depth increased from 12 inches prior to this storm, to 19 inches.   Beckley saw their snow depth go from 7 inches before the storm, to 17 inches.  On a mountain near Richwood, their snow depth went from 8 inches before the storm, to 19 inches by dawn on the 11th.  Elkins new snow accumulation was 6 inches, with a total snow depth of 13 inches at the end of this storm.    In the river valley at Webster Springs, a 6 inch snow accumulation was also measured.

The 2nd prolonged period of snow showers in the later half of February occurred from late on Wednesday the 24th into early on Sunday the 28th.||The event started as a strong upper air disturbance crossed the area late on the 24th into Thursday the 25th.  Winds increased late on the 25th into midday Friday the 26th, as the surface storm intensified along the coast.  The snow showers continued on the 26th.  Blowing snow and frequent wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph caused near blizzard conditions across the open high terrain of Randolph, Webster, Nicholas, and Pocahontas Counties.  A peak gust of 47 mph was noted on the mountaintop at Snowshoe.  Route 32 and 33 were briefly closed down in northeastern Randolph County by the poor travel conditions.  Winds subsided by late afternoon on the 26th.||Late on Friday the 26th into Saturday the 27th, deeper moisture wrapped south, around the nearly stationary storm that was in the vicinity of New York City.   The lowland counties from Pleasants, Tyler, Doddridge, and Harrison, on south to Kanawha and Boone saw their peak in the snow showers at this time.  Snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches occurred there, in about a 12 hour period.||The storm slowly weakened on Saturday the 27th.    ||Over the high mountains a total snowfall of 2 to 3 feet occurred over a 3 day period.  One to 2 feet of snow fell along many of the river valleys in the mountainous counties over the same period.  Of course, due to settling of the deep snow pack, the increase in the snow depth was less than the snowfall.   Pickens reported a 38 inch snowfall.  Their snow depth went from 12 inches prior to the storm, to 30 inches.  The mountain observer near Richwood had a 30 inch snowfall, with their snow depth increasing from 8 inches to 32 inches.  Valley Head had around 26 inches of snow fall with their snow depth increasing from 7 inches to 24 inches.  Glady had a 24 inch snowfall.  The snow depth at Glady was 12 inches prior to the storm.  The snow depth at Glady was 28 inches at its conclusion.  Buckhannon had a 23 inches of snow fall.  Their snow depth went from 4 inches to 23 inches.  Elkins had a 16 inch snow fall.  The snow depth at Elkins went from 7 inches prior to the storm, to 17 inches at the conclusion.  Beckley also had over a foot of snowfall with their snow depth increasing from 2 inches to 14 inches. ||The month ended with over 100 inches of snowfall at Snowshoe.  Several other mountain locations had around 75 inches or more of snowfall in February.  The monthly snowfall totals of 45 inches at Beckley, 44 inches at Elkins, 38 inches at Clarksburg, and nearly 26 inches at Charleston were the most on record for the month of February at those sites.

One to 2 feet of snow fell along many of the river valleys in the mountainous counties over the same period.  Of course, due to settling of the deep snow pack, the increase in the snow depth was less than the snowfall.   Pickens reported a 38 inch snowfall.  Their snow depth went from 12 inches prior to the storm, to 30 inches.  The mountain observer near Richwood had a 30 inch snowfall, with their snow depth increasing from 8 inches to 32 inches.  Valley Head had around 26 inches of snow fall with their snow depth increasing from 7 inches to 24 inches.  Glady had a 24 inch snowfall.  The snow depth at Glady was 12 inches prior to the storm.  The snow depth at Glady was 28 inches at its conclusion.  Buckhannon had a 23 inches of snow fall.  Their snow depth went from 4 inches to 23 inches.  Elkins had a 16 inch snow fall.  The snow depth at Elkins went from 7 inches prior to the storm, to 17 inches at the conclusion.  Beckley also had over a foot of snowfall with their snow depth increasing from 2 inches to 14 inches. ||The month ended with over 100 inches of snowfall at Snowshoe. A wintry mix of precipitation arrived during the morning of Tuesday the 9th.  However, the mixture changed to mostly rain for the afternoon, as the low pressure center lifted northeast toward Ohio.  The artcic cold front swept through around 0000E on Wednesday the 10th.  The rain had changed to snow Tuesday evening, prior to the frontal passage.  ||As the coastal storm strengthened during the daylight hours of Wednesday the 10th, wind blown snow showers were seen over West Virginia.  Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph were felt.  The snow showers diminished that evening over the lowlands.  However, the snow showers did not diminish until early on the 11th for the mountainous counties. ||The lowlands saw widespread accumulations of 2 to 5 inches over 24 hours.  The heavy snow was limited to the mountainous counties.  Snow accumulations of 6 to 12 inches were common. The extreme case was at Kumbrabow State Forest, where the snow depth increased from 15 inches prior to the storm, to 29 inches.  Pickens snow depth went from 6 inches to 15 inches.  Glady snow depth increased from 12 inches prior to this storm, to 19 inches.   Beckley saw their snow depth go from 7 inches before the storm, to 17 inches.  On a mountain near Richwood, their snow depth went from 8 inches before the storm, to 19 inches by dawn on the 11th.  Elkins new snow accumulation was 6 inches, with a total snow depth of 13 inches at the end of this storm.    In the river valley at Webster Springs, a 6 inch snow accumulation was also measured.

A continuous rain on Friday the 5th became heavier in the evening over the southwestern counties of West Virginia.  Twelve hour rains of over an inch caused minor stream flooding along roads across portions of Lincoln, Putnam, Cabell, Kanawha, Roane, Jackson, Mason, and Calhoun.   The rain changed to sleet then wet snow overnight, or late on the 5th into the 6th.    ||An exception was Pocahontas County, where the easterly low level flow kept the precipitation as mostly snow.  At Snowshoe the snow depth went from 10 inches prior to the storm to 27 inches at the conclusion. At Marlinton the snow depth went from 7 inches to 20 inches.  At Bartow the snow depth went from 5 inches to 13 inches.  ||The other exception was across Fayette and Raleigh Counties in the southern mountains of West Virginia.  Here, pockets of freezing rain fell during the daylight hours on the 5th, before changing to snow that night.  The ice from the freezing rain accumulated to around a quarter of an inch.  The snow accumulations were mostly |2 to 4 inches.||The heavy wet snow caused power outages across the northern lowland counties of West Virginia.  On the 11th, several days after the storm, Harrison County still had hundreds of structures without power.   Clarksburg snow depth went from zero prior to the storm to 11 inches.   Grafton had a 9 inch accumulation.  Harrisville had an 8 inch snow accumulation, while Middlebourne measured 7 inches.  Elkins had a 6 inch snow accumulation.||Further south, toward Glenville and Sutton, snow accumulations of 5 to 6 inches were more common.||In the central mountain counties of Nicholas and Webster, not enough freezing rain or snow fell to verify the winter storm warning||Since the storm had been predicted well in advance, Governor Joe Manchin declared a state of emergency at the very onset of the storm, on the 5th.

As an area of strong low pressure approached from the west, southeast winds in advance of it strengthen. The influence of terrain helped to bring winds 45 to 60 mph to the surface. Combined with very wet soil, these winds helped to topple some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging wind gusts brought a tree down on Sharps Turn Road four miles east of Princeton. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold air and moisture from the Great Lakes triggered snow showers for locations along and west of the Allegheny front. The heaviest snow fell on the morning and afternoon hours of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was estimated around three inches near Riverton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold air and moisture from the Great Lakes triggered snow showers for locations along and west of the Allegheny front. The heaviest snow fell on the morning and afternoon hours of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 7.0 inches in Bayard and 6.0 inches near Mount Storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the Ohio Valley on the 2nd before emerging off the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the early morning hours of the 3rd. The forcing from the low combined with cold air already in place to produce snow across eastern West Virginia during late afternoon hours on the 2nd into the early morning hours of the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bayard received six inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low passed through the Mid-Atlantic on the night of the 24th. The upper-level low combined with energy in the southern branch of the jet stream, causing low pressure to rapidly intensify over New England on the 25th into the 26th. Low pressure remained nearly stationary before finally weakening and moving off to the east on the 28th.||A northwest flow around the low triggered a prolonged period of upslope snow for locations along and west of the Allegheny Front. Gusty winds and locally heavy snow reduced visibility to near zero at times, especially on the night of the 25th into the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of wind gusts over 35 mph along with visibility below one quarter mile due to snow and blowing snow resulted in blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a cold and snowy February, a switch to a warmer pattern began during the second week of the month.  This started the runoff from a deep snow pack over the West Virginia mountainous counties.  A widespread 2 to 4 inches of water resided in the snow pack with some ridgetops exceeding 6 inches of water in the snow.  ||Flood concerns were high preceding the event, but mainly for the central and northern mountain counties of the state.  The deepest snow cover still resided across the high terrain in those counties.  Prior to the heavy rain, the snow cover over Fayette and Raleigh Counties had already melted away.  However, the ground was left soggy and primed to allow for a quick runoff.||Governor Manchin even declared a state of emergency before any rain arrived. ||An increasing southeast flow developed on Friday the 12th with dew points climbing into the mid and upper 40s.  The support for the showers was provided by a large mid and upper air low circulation, shifting east into the Tennessee Valley. |   |Showers developed early that evening, then moved northwest through the southern mountains of West Virginia into the Coal and Kanawha River Valleys.  A few thunderstorms were embedded within the heavier showers.  Training of these cells occurred in narrow bands oriented in an arch from southeast to northwest. ||Luckily, the heavy rain never transferred further north, where the mountain snow pack was still melting. ||In a 6 hour period ending at 0100E on the 13th, the Raleigh County Airport received 2.58 inches of rain, while Charleston had 0.80 inches.   By 0700E on the 13th, Beckley had a 3 inch rain total.   Oak Hill in Fayette County had 2.95 inches ending at dawn on the 13th.  The cooperative observer in Beckley measured 2.12 inches.  The Huff Knob automated rain gauge near Ghent measured 2.76 inches in the 12 hour overnight period.  On Tams Mountain, west of Sophia, another automatic rain gauge measured 2.1 inches in 12 hours.  Oceana of northern Wyoming County measured 2.3 inches.  Amounts around 2 inches in 12 hours were common through the Big Coal River Valley to around Charleston.  An unofficial spotter report of 3.6 inches was received just 1 mile southeast of downtown Beckley.  Further north, rain amounts around an inch reached into Nicholas County.  ||After dawn on the 13th, only spotty light rain lingered in the cooler air.  ||Major small stream flooding was widespread in Raleigh and Fayette Counties.  There were 2 direct fatalities from Raleigh County.  Flooding of less severity occurred in  Kanawha, Nicholas, Boone, and Wyoming Counties.  ||During the daylight hours on the 13th, the Kanawha and Coal Rivers rose rapidly and caused backwater flooding.  For example, the mouth of Paint Creek flooded in the Hansford and Pratt vicinity.  Later that night, the Coal River crested at 25.5 feet at Tornado, slightly over its 25 foot flood stage.  The crest on the Kanawha at Charleston was 29 feet, just under the 30 foot flood stage.||The American Red Cross served over 1000 meals and around 900 snacks in the affected flood zone.  They also distributed over 400 clean-up kits.  The Salvation Army and various church groups also came to the aid of the local citizens.  ||A federal disaster was declared that included Raleigh, Fayette, and Kanawha Counties.  See FEMA disaster number 1893 for more details.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major stream flooding along such streams as Piney, Beaver, Little Beaver, Little Whitestick, and the headwaters of Dunloup Creek, including Maple Fork. Damage was less along the Marsh Fork and Clear Fork drainage in the western portion of the county. ||A 59 year old female turnpike worker was returning home from the evening shift.  She left her vehicle parked and attempted to walk through the flood waters of Maple Fork west of Bradley.  She was swept away and drowned.||The majority of the property damage in Raleigh County was along the urbanized Beaver Creek, Little Beaver Creek, and Piney Creek.  This would be in the vicinity of the communities of Beaver and Glen Morgan.  A stream gauge was located along Piney Creek upstream from the mouth of Beaver Creek.  The water level at this gauge rose from about 3 feet to a record high of 11.4 feet around 0430E on the 13th.||One resident along Beaver Creek said, I've lived here for 24 years and never seen anything like this.  Other residents along the lower reaches of Beaver Creek sought refuge in their attics and called 911 for help.  A swift water rescue team from the Glasgow Fire Department was working rescues along Beaver Creek.  The boat struck something in the water, lost its steering and crashed into a bridge.  The boat capsized, throwing 3 firefighters into the water.  Two were able to reach safety.  However, a 32 year old male firefighter was swept away.  His body was not found until the 19th along Piney Creek.  Beaver Creek flows into Piney Creek before emptying into the Kanawha River.       ||Many culverts under roadbeds could not handle the flow.  A few were completely washed out. ||Preliminary data indicated 29 homes were destroyed in Raleigh County, with major damage to 34 other homes.  Dozens of vehicles were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northwest flow around departing low pressure triggered upslope snow showers for locations along and west of the Allegheny front in eastern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 7.0 inches about six miles west-southwest of Cherry Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low presure moved east across the Ohio Valley the afternoon of the 15th spreading snow across much of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Snow continued in northwest flow behind the storm into the 18th with storm totals in the ridges of northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland from 12 to 18 inches. Some snow amounts across eastern Ohio were from 6 to 9 inches, with 6 to 10 inches in the ridges of Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. A general storm total of 4 to 7 inches was reported across the remainder of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.

A potent area of low pressure tracked through the Midwest on the 9th. As the low approached the Mid-Atlantic coast, this system phased with energy in the southern branch of the jet stream to cause strong low pressure to develop just off the Delmarva peninsula. The low continued to rapidly strengthen as it moved off to our northeast on the 10th. ||The system spread snow across West Virginia that began on the 9th and lasted into the 10th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall averaged between 7 and 12 inches near Romney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure strengthened over the central portion of the nation on the fifth of February. The storm system slowly moved through the Mid-Atlantic during the night of the 5th before redeveloping off the Mid-Atlantic coast on 6th. The storm system finally moved away from the area on the night of the 6th.||Strong high pressure continued to pump in plenty of cold air across the region for the entire event. Due to the slow movement of the storm, there was a prolonged period of precipitation. The storm system ushered in copious amounts of moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The deep moisture combined with the forcing from the storm system to bring a period of heavy precipitation to the area on the night of the 5th through the daytime hours on the 6th. Precipitation finally ended during the evening hours of the 6th as the storm system moved away. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of snow due to the cold air that was already in place.||Major snow accumulations were reported throughout the state of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts between 23 and 30 inches were received across the county.||A local newspaper reported power outages across the county. There was also a report of a roof collapse due to the weight of the snow in southern Berkeley County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a cold and snowy February, a switch to a warmer pattern began during the second week of the month.  This started the runoff from a deep snow pack over the West Virginia mountainous counties.  A widespread 2 to 4 inches of water resided in the snow pack with some ridgetops exceeding 6 inches of water in the snow.  ||Flood concerns were high preceding the event, but mainly for the central and northern mountain counties of the state.  The deepest snow cover still resided across the high terrain in those counties.  Prior to the heavy rain, the snow cover over Fayette and Raleigh Counties had already melted away.  However, the ground was left soggy and primed to allow for a quick runoff.||Governor Manchin even declared a state of emergency before any rain arrived. ||An increasing southeast flow developed on Friday the 12th with dew points climbing into the mid and upper 40s.  The support for the showers was provided by a large mid and upper air low circulation, shifting east into the Tennessee Valley. |   |Showers developed early that evening, then moved northwest through the southern mountains of West Virginia into the Coal and Kanawha River Valleys.  A few thunderstorms were embedded within the heavier showers.  Training of these cells occurred in narrow bands oriented in an arch from southeast to northwest. ||Luckily, the heavy rain never transferred further north, where the mountain snow pack was still melting. ||In a 6 hour period ending at 0100E on the 13th, the Raleigh County Airport received 2.58 inches of rain, while Charleston had 0.80 inches.   By 0700E on the 13th, Beckley had a 3 inch rain total.   Oak Hill in Fayette County had 2.95 inches ending at dawn on the 13th.  The cooperative observer in Beckley measured 2.12 inches.  The Huff Knob automated rain gauge near Ghent measured 2.76 inches in the 12 hour overnight period.  On Tams Mountain, west of Sophia, another automatic rain gauge measured 2.1 inches in 12 hours.  Oceana of northern Wyoming County measured 2.3 inches.  Amounts around 2 inches in 12 hours were common through the Big Coal River Valley to around Charleston.  An unofficial spotter report of 3.6 inches was received just 1 mile southeast of downtown Beckley.  Further north, rain amounts around an inch reached into Nicholas County.  ||After dawn on the 13th, only spotty light rain lingered in the cooler air.  ||Major small stream flooding was widespread in Raleigh and Fayette Counties.  There were 2 direct fatalities from Raleigh County.  Flooding of less severity occurred in  Kanawha, Nicholas, Boone, and Wyoming Counties.  ||During the daylight hours on the 13th, the Kanawha and Coal Rivers rose rapidly and caused backwater flooding.  For example, the mouth of Paint Creek flooded in the Hansford and Pratt vicinity.  Later that night, the Coal River crested at 25.5 feet at Tornado, slightly over its 25 foot flood stage.  The crest on the Kanawha at Charleston was 29 feet, just under the 30 foot flood stage.||The American Red Cross served over 1000 meals and around 900 snacks in the affected flood zone.  They also distributed over 400 clean-up kits.  The Salvation Army and various church groups also came to the aid of the local citizens.  ||A federal disaster was declared that included Raleigh, Fayette, and Kanawha Counties.  See FEMA disaster number 1893 for more details.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small stream flooding was common across the southern and western portions of the county.  Streams such as Paint Creek, White Oak Creek , and Dunloup Creek flooded.  This affected such communities as Pax, Mount Hope, Kilsyth, and Glen Jean.  Water rescues were done.  A 92 year old woman in Pax went under the water after leaving a vehicle.  She was rescued.  ||No homes were destroyed in Fayette County, but 38 homes had major damage. Several vehicles were flooded.  Damage to the public infrastructure was estimated at around a half million dollars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams started to flood before 0200E.  Shortly after 0200E, a vehicle was submerged in Smith Creek and a wrecker was unable to retrieve it.  Rocky Fork and Fisher Branch flooded roads northeast of Cross Lanes.||The worst small stream flooding was at the mouth of Paint Creek in the Hansford to Pratt vicinity. An unofficial spotter report of 3.6 inches was received just 1 mile southeast of downtown Beckley.  Further north, rain amounts around an inch reached into Nicholas County.  ||After dawn on the 13th, only spotty light rain lingered in the cooler air.  ||Major small stream flooding was widespread in Raleigh and Fayette Counties.  There were 2 direct fatalities from Raleigh County.  Flooding of less severity occurred in  Kanawha, Nicholas, Boone, and Wyoming Counties.  ||During the daylight hours on the 13th, the Kanawha and Coal Rivers rose rapidly and caused backwater flooding.  For example, the mouth of Paint Creek flooded in the Hansford and Pratt vicinity.  Later that night, the Coal River crested at 25.5 feet at Tornado, slightly over its 25 foot flood stage.  The crest on the Kanawha at Charleston was 29 feet, just under the 30 foot flood stage.||The American Red Cross served over 1000 meals and around 900 snacks in the affected flood zone.  They also distributed over 400 clean-up kits.  The Salvation Army and various church groups also came to the aid of the local citizens.  ||A federal disaster was declared that included Raleigh, Fayette, and Kanawha Counties.  See FEMA disaster number 1893 for more details.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams feeding into the upper Guyandotte River flooded in the early morning, followed by minor overflow of the upper Guyandotte River.  Roads were blocked by high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure moving north from the Gulf of Mexico on February 5th combined with low pressure across the Ohio Valley and intensified over the Mid-Atlantic states. This produced an historic snow storm across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and eastern Ohio from the afternoon of February 5th to the afternoon of February 6th. Over 2 feet of snow fell across portions of Garrett county Maryland, and in Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, with locally 3 feet in some locations. Storm totals ranged from 37 inches in Friendsville, MD to 22 inches in Terra Alta, WV. Across Pennsylvania, 18 to 24 inches of snow fell roughly between an area from SR 422 and I-70, with 21.1 inches of snow falling in Pittsburgh. This was the 4th largest snowfall on record in Pittsburgh. Over 2 feet of snow fell in the ridges of Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. Along and north of I-80 in Pennsylvania 6 to 18 inches of snow fell. South of I-70 in Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches. North of I-70, in eastern Ohio, snow accumulated 12 to 18 inches. The snow was wet and heavy in Pittsburgh and areas south, bringing down trees and power lines with around 200,000 people without power at some point after the storm. Roads were not passable for 2 to 3 days in some locations, and power was not restored to some homes until 3 days after the storm.

A complex low pressure system situated over the Ohio Valley combined with another low forming off the southeast coast to bring abundant moisture into the area. A band of thunderstorms developed and tracked northeast into Southern West Virginia during the evening of March 12th. A southeast flow helped enhance the rainfall over Mercer County. An existing snow pack in western Greenbrier county also helped contribute to the water runoff and flooding during the early morning hours of March 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Virginia Department of Transportation reported 35 and 40 roads were closed in Mercer county due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level low slowly tracked through the central portion of the country on the 25th. A southwest flow ahead of this system ushered in warm and humid air. A cold front dropped into the region from the northeast during the afternoon and evening hours of the 25th. The cold front combined with plenty of instability for showers and thunderstorms to develop. Thunderstorms dumped locally heavy amounts of rain which led to an isolated flash flooding event in Hampshire County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing into a house on Tannery Road near Capon Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure over the Great Lakes slowly tracked to our north on the 8th. The cold front associated with the low moved into the region during the afternoon and evening hours. A moist southerly flow ahead of the front provided instability. The instability combined with the forcing from the front to trigger showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours of the 8th. Due to strong winds aloft, an isolated thunderstorm did produce gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was partially collapsed due to gusty winds in a thunderstorm. Wind gusts were estimated to be around 45 knots.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported on Dogwood Road about two miles west of Middleway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few showers and thunderstorms developed during the morning hours, well ahead of a surface warm front in Kentucky and southwest Virginia.  ||After temperatures reached into the upper 70s and lower 80s, along with dew points near 60, more numerous showers and thunderstorms formed by late afternoon.  Some mid level disturbances and a westerly elevated inflow, helped the convection survive well into the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a petroleum container at a water treatment facility.  An oil fire was ignited that caused considerable damage.  Since there was a possibility of an explosion, the nearby area was evacuated.  ||At the Upshur County Regional Airport, lightning struck one of the ramps.  It created several holes in the asphalt.  ||An elderly woman suffered minor injuries from being hit by lightning while looking out the screen door of her Gate Street home in Buckhannon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland ahead of a cold front moving east across the Ohio Valley. One long lived supercell produced large hail across northern West Virginia and Garrett county, Maryland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the area on January 28th.  Behind this front cold air was left in its wake. On the 29th, an area of low pressure moved the northern edge of the Gulf of Mexico before heading north and strengthening along the eastern coast of the U.S. on the 30th. This series of events allowed for plenty of moisture to fall as snow across the area with total accumulations ranging from the five to fourteen inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the county totaled 9.8 inches in Ballard and 7.2 inches in Union.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the county totaled 14.0 inches in White Sulphur, 9.5 inches in Alderson, 8.1 inches in Lewisburg, 8.0 inches in Friars Hill, 6.0 inches in Quinwood and Renicks Valley, and 5.5 inches in McRoss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow occurred along the west facing slopes of the higher elevations of the western portions of Greenbrier County West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 14 inches of snow fell in Quinwood West Virginia and 10.5 inches fell in Anjean West Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With low pressure stalled well north of the region, strong northwest winds helped to generate another heavy snow event across the higher terrain of western Greenbrier County West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the higher elevations of the western portion of Greenbrier County. A total of 14 inches fell at Quinwood, West Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly flow, ahead of a cold front, transported very moist air through Tennessee and Kentucky.  Surface dew points were in the mid and upper 60s.  Wave after wave in the mid and upper levels helped trigger widespread showers, with embedded thunderstorms.  These training rains entered northeast Kentucky and southeast Ohio during the morning of May 2nd.  However, the rain was slow to move east, not becoming steady until late afternoon for portions of western West Virginia. The heaviest rains fell during the evening hours to after midnight.  However, light to moderate rain lingered until the predawn hours of Monday the 3rd.||The rain was not as heavy in West Virginia, when compared to counties to the west, in northeast Kentucky and southeast Ohio.  The heaviest rain rates in West Virginia were on the order of .5 to 1 inch in less than 3 hours.||Rain totals of 2.25 to 3.0 inches were widespread along the Ohio River counties, with a few locations in Cabell and northwestern Mason County exceeding 3.5 inches.  The 24 hours rain amount at the cooperative observer in Huntington was 3.63 inches.  However, further east in Cabell County, rain amounts were less.  Pea Ridge measured 2.2 inches of rain. The Tri State Airport in northern Wayne County had 3.1 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Krout Creek flooded Spring Valley Drive, but damage to homes was minimal. Other roads in the Kenova and Ceredo vicinity were flooded, mostly from poor drainage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moving off the South Carolina coast combined with an upper level disturbance moving out of the Ohio Valley producing areas of light to moderate snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall overspread the area during the morning and continued for much of the day. Accumulations were generally in the 4 to 6 inch range across Greenbrier County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly staitonary front was across southeast Ohio and northern West Virginia. During the afternoon and early evening, repetitive showers and thunderstorms moved west to east in West Virginia.  A spotter reported 2.5 inches of rain in Sissonville of northern Kanawha County.  On Black Mountain in Pocahontas County, 2.3 inches fell in less than 3 hours.  Amounts of around 2 inches likely fell just southwest of Oak Hill in Fayette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams from the west side of Charleston to Sissionville flooded and closed roads.  This included the headwaters of Kanawha Two Mile.  Four vehicles were stranded in high water near the Pocatalico exit off of Interstate 77.   No dwellings were reported flooded.

An upper level low centered overhead, combined with a deepening coastal low brought cold air and strong northwest winds to the area. This resulted in significant upslope snow showers across the higher elevations of southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prolonged upslope snow produced significant accumulations across the western slopes. As much as 9 inches fell in western areas with less to the east. Significant blowing and drifting of the snow produced near blizzard conditions at times.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prolonged upslope snow produced significant accumulations across the western slopes. As much as 25 to 38 inches fell over the 4 day span across the western slopes near Mcross and Hines. Significant blowing and drifting of the snow produced near blizzard conditions at times. Drifts as high as high as 5 feet were reported. Close to 11 inches fell further east near Lewisburg over this prolonged period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of convection affected West Virginia from late Sunday afternoon on the 16th into Tuesday afternoon the 18th.  ||Initially, an easterly flow into the southern and central mountainous counties, helped trigger showers and thunderstorms late Sunday afternoon.  Rains of 2 to 3 inches fell around Beavers and Glen Morgan in Raleigh County, plus near Meadow Bridge in Fayette County, causing minor flash flooding.||On Monday the 17th, a strong mid level low was over Indiana with its surface reflection in Ohio.  The associated front moved into West Virginia during the afternoon.  Short lines of thunderstorms formed parallel to their movement.  Local downpours caused flash flooding when these short lines trained over the same creek basins.  Some large hail occurred in Wyoming County.||On Tuesday, as the mid level low crept closer, scattered thunderstorms did form during the afternoon.  Some minor flooding occurred during this final round of convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This area had received heavy rain 5 days earlier on the 12th.  Another round of showers fell on the 14th.  The ground was wetter than normal.  Rain estimates of 2 to 3 inches fell in an hour. ||The most impact was along small tributaries to Rocky Fork, including Fishers Branch.  An unnamed tributary creek along Route 501 flooded backyards and around homes east of Cross Lanes.  Near the junction of Route 501 and 622, 2 homes were severely flooded on a private road called Valley Hill Drive. A private culvert could not handle the water.  The water could only flow around the culvert on one side.  The high water knocked down fences, went over the road and straight through 2 rental homes.  Water was several feet deep in those 2 homes.  ||Rocky Branch flooded sections of Route 622.  The headwaters of Martins Branch flooded a few homes, outbuildings and garages.  Numerous streams between Pocatalico and Sissonville flooded, including Legg Fork.  The Pocatalico Community Church had several feet of water in its basement.  Even Flinn Elementary School got a few inches of water inside the building.  The school is not near a stream.  The principal said, normally it's not even a possibility that we'll get flooded.  We weren't prepared for all of this water.  The downpour over the wet soils caused runoff to flow down an urbanized hillside.  A drain outside the school was clogged, allowing the water to come inside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the region.  In the wake of the cold front, strong wind developed. Some of these winds were strong enough to down some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds in the wake of a cold front downed a tree in Ronceverte. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple, slow moving, heavy rain producing thunderstorms progressed across the same regions over a short period of time. The large volume of water in narrow creek basins prompted significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused flash flooding to take place. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly flow, ahead of a cold front, transported very moist air through Tennessee and Kentucky.  Surface dew points were in the mid and upper 60s.  Wave after wave in the mid and upper levels helped trigger widespread showers, with embedded thunderstorms.  These training rains entered northeast Kentucky and southeast Ohio during the morning of May 2nd.  However, the rain was slow to move east, not becoming steady until late afternoon for portions of western West Virginia. The heaviest rains fell during the evening hours to after midnight.  However, light to moderate rain lingered until the predawn hours of Monday the 3rd.||The rain was not as heavy in West Virginia, when compared to counties to the west, in northeast Kentucky and southeast Ohio.  The heaviest rain rates in West Virginia were on the order of .5 to 1 inch in less than 3 hours.||Rain totals of 2.25 to 3.0 inches were widespread along the Ohio River counties, with a few locations in Cabell and northwestern Mason County exceeding 3.5 inches.  The 24 hours rain amount at the cooperative observer in Huntington was 3.63 inches.  However, further east in Cabell County, rain amounts were less.  Pea Ridge measured 2.2 inches of rain. The Tri State Airport in northern Wayne County had 3.1 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous small creeks flooded roads.  Walker Creek was one example. A small bridge was damaged across Dry Run near Williamstown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain estimates of 2 to 3 inches of rain were indicated around Meadow Bridge.  Laurel Branch and Lively Branch flooded roads.  Evacuations were done as a precaution.  Sewell Creek also flooded around Bellwood.

Thunderstorms formed over the mountains in a weak flow.  Outflow boundaries helped trigger new convection on the perimeter of the existing storms.  Localized downpours caused 2.6 inches of rain at Valley Head in a few hours. However, no significant flooding occurred.  One storm did cause localized wind damage in eastern Harrison County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind blew off a portion of a tin roof to an indoor riding barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front pushed south into the region and stalled along the crest of the Blue Ridge along a north to south orientation. During the afternoon and early evening, numerous thunderstorms developed along and near the front. Some of these produced damaging wind and hail along with flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from dime to quarter size.
