Multiple, slow moving, heavy rain producing thunderstorms progressed across the same regions over a short period of time. The large volume of water in narrow creek basins prompted significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rains fell along the Meadow Creek basin between Meadow Creek and Sandstone.  A local unofficial rainfall measurement was 5.25 inches in one hour.  The heavy rain caused Meadow Creek to leave its banks and flow across Route 7.  The downpour also caused mud and rock slides to take place along Route 7.  Entire trees were washed down Meadow Creek, several homes had water damage, and many driveways and roads were closed. Damage values are estimated.

During the afternoon and early evening, numerous thunderstorms developed along and near the front. Some of these produced damaging wind and hail along with flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash water waters washed out Hans Creek Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved from the Gulf Coast to off the North Carolina coast. A secondary low moved over Kentucky, bringing a nose of warm air in aloft. This led to a mixture of snow, sleet, freezing rain, and rain across southeast West Virginia, with many areas seeing significant snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain moved into the area early Friday and continued through the day. The most snow fell across the eastern part of the county where the cold air held in the longest. Further west warmer air moved in causing a mixed bag of precipitation, and eventually just rain. Accumulations of 5 to 8 inches were common across the eastern half of the county, with less to the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved from the Gulf Coast to off the North Carolina coast. A secondary low moved over Kentucky, bringing a nose of warm air in aloft. This led to a mixture of snow, sleet, freezing rain, and rain across southeast West Virginia, with many areas seeing significant snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain moved into the area early Friday and continued through the day. Accumulations of near 8 inches were common across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved from the Gulf Coast to off the North Carolina coast. A secondary low moved over Kentucky, bringing a nose of warm air in aloft. This led to a mixture of snow, sleet, freezing rain, and rain across southeast West Virginia, with many areas seeing significant snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow moved into the county early on Friday and continued through early Saturday afternoon. The lower elevations were near freezing for part of the storm, allowing some sleet, freezing rain, and rain to mix in at times. The highest snow accumulations were in the higher elevations where it was coldest. Snow amounts ranged from around 9 inches in the lower elevations to as much as 24 inches near Quinwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from the Mississippi coast to off the Carolina coast. At the same time another low moved through the Ohio Valley, putting southeast West Virginia in the middle of the two. An area of mixed precipitation moved across the area with light to moderate accumulations. The coastal low deepened on Wednesday bringing gusty winds and significant upslope snow showers to the higher elevations of southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of snow moved into the county early on the 9th, before mixing with sleet and rain during the afternoon. By early on the 10th cold northwest winds gusting up to 45 mph moved into the area producing significant upslope snows. The wind and snow combined to produce near blizzard conditions at times, creating significant drifting and hazardous travel conditions. Total snow ranged from 8 inches in Princeton to 15 inches in Lashmeet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of snow moved into the county early on the 9th, before mixing with sleet and rain during the afternoon. By early on the 10th strong cold northwest winds moved into the area producing significant upslope snows. The wind and snow combined to produce near blizzard conditions at times, creating significant drifting and hazardous travel conditions. Total snow was around 7 inches in Lewisburg and other lower elevation areas. Across the western slopes accumulations around 15 inches were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough centered overhead brought cold northwest winds into the area, bringing significant upslope snows to the normally prone western slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged upslope snow event produced accumulations as high as two feet on the western slopes, with lower amounts near 7 inches across the eastern part of the county. Considerable blowing and drifting of the snow was also common with gusty northwest winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough centered overhead brought cold northwest winds into the area, bringing significant upslope snows to the normally prone western slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged upslope snow event produced accumulations of 4 to 7 inches across the county, with the highest amounts across the higher elevations. Considerable blowing and drifting of the snow was also common with gusty northwest winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low over the Ohio Valley provided plenty of cold air aloft while a southerly flow ahead of this system ushered in warm and moist air from the south at the surface. The combination of the warm low-levels and the colder air aloft provided enough instability for showers and thunderstorms to develop. An isolated thunderstorm did produce large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported near Reeses Mill.

This led to a mixture of snow, sleet, freezing rain, and rain across southeast West Virginia, with many areas seeing significant snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain moved into the area early Friday and continued through the day. The most snow fell across the eastern part of the county, near Bluefield, where the cold air held in the longest. Further west warmer air moved in causing a mixed bag of precipitation, and eventually just rain. Accumulations of 5 to 8 inches were common across the eastern half of the county, with less to the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moving off the South Carolina coast combined with an upper level disturbance moving out of the Ohio Valley producing areas of light to moderate snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall overspread the area during the morning and continued for much of the day. Accumulations were generally in the 3 to 6 inch range across Monroe county.

Some mid level disturbances and a westerly elevated inflow, helped the convection survive well into the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball size hail fell at Prince.

Storms fired up over the mountains south of a front stalled out over the Ohio Valley. There were reports of both large hail and scattered wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down on Muddy Creek Mountain Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the western Atlantic provided a southwest flow which pumped in warm and moist air from the Gulf of Mexico. An upper-level disturbance passed through in the westerly flow aloft. An isolated thunderstorm did produce damaging wind due to moderate amounts of instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust was estimated to be around 60 mph near West Points.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest wind flow of hot and humid air helped induce scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms. One storm pulsed to a stronger level in Boyd County Kentucky and moved into West Virginia.  Another storm pulsed to a stronger level over the southern mountains of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto vehicles at the intersection of 9th Avenue and 10th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed during the afternoon over southern West Virginnia in advance of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down across northern Raleigh County.  The main concentration was in the town of Bradley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was another hot and humid June day.  Leftover boundaries from morning convection helped form more thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down along Route 44.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was another hot and humid June day.  Leftover boundaries from morning convection helped form more thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elementary school roof was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was another hot and humid June day.  Leftover boundaries from morning convection helped form more thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Pleasant Ridge Road and Sellers Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front passed through eastern West Virginia on the 28th. Showers and thunderstorms developed near the boundary. Warm and humid air ahead of the front lead to moderate amounts of instability. An isolated thunderstorm produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were down along North River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well south of a frontal boundary, a moist and unstable air mass resided over the Ohio Valley and Central Appalachians.   The ground was already wetter than normal from previous rains.  A southwest and west wind along with daytime heating formed showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 20th.   Overnight, more storms formed along a leftover east to west boundary.  That allowed for repetitive showers from portions of northeast Kentucky into extreme southern Ohio and western West Virginia.||After some severe thunderstorms during the afternoon, water issues were the main concern overnight.  The main period of rain was from mid afternoon on the 20th into the predawn hours of the 21st.  Rain amounts of 3 to 4 inches were common in parts of Wayne, Cabell, Lincoln, Putnam, and Kanawha Counties.  The city of Wayne measured 3.84 inches of rain in the 24 hours ending at dawn on the 21st, with 1.75 inches falling in 2 hours.   Pea Ridge and Saint Albans both measured around 3.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down, some fell onto power lines.

The city of Wayne measured 3.84 inches of rain in the 24 hours ending at dawn on the 21st, with 1.75 inches falling in 2 hours.   Pea Ridge and Saint Albans both measured around 3.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The headwaters of Peters Creeks flooded portions of Route 39.  Water got around about a dozen homes.  Private bridges were damaged.

Showers and thunderstorms formed in southeast Ohio during the late morning on the 24th, ahead of a surface cold front.  Winds aloft were stronger than average for late June.  A 30 to 35 mph flow existed at 3 to 10 thousand feet above the ground.  The storms intensified as they moved across northern West Virginia.  Wet microbursts occurred near Weston in Lewis County.  The storms were not quite as strong across southern West Virginia.  Local downpours were common, with rain rates of 1 to 2 inches in less than an hour.  Some training of the cells produced 2 to 3 inches of rain in Upshur County, but flooding was insignificant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell across power lines along Heizer Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several mid level disturbances acted on instability and high precipitable water to result in repetitive showers and thunderstorms.  One complex of showers and thunderstorms moved out of southern Ohio into West Virginia during the early afternoon on Saturday the 12th.  It helped trigger heavier rains on its southern and southwestern flank as the afternoon continued.  A west to east oriented train of downpours developed.  This train of heavy rain moved through Logan, northeastern Mingo, Wyoming Counties during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 12th.  The downpours eventually sank south into northern McDowell County later that same  evening.  The rain diminished between 0000E and 0300E on Sunday the 13th.  ||Rain amounts of 3 to 5 inches occurred in about a 12 to 15 hour time frame.  Some preliminary rain amounts included R. D. Bailey Lake with 4.82 inches, Man had 4.81 inches, Pineville 4.25 inches, and Oceana 3.33 inches.     ||At the onset of this rain, the ground was wetter than normal.  Rains had been occurring at intervals of every 2 or 3 days during the preceding days in June.||Small stream flooding was widespread.  With the steep terrain, much of the damage was not caused directly by stream flooding, but rather the runoff directly down the mountain slopes.   Mud, rocks, and vegetation came off the slopes and into dwellings.  In some cases shoving buildings off their foundations.  Roads and bridges were closed and damaged.||A strong rise occurred along the upper Guyandotte River.  Even downstream of the R. D. Bailey Dam, the level in the community of Man crested at 16.6 feet around 0230E on the 13th, slightly over its 15 foot estimated flood stage.  The river crested just below the 23 foot stage in the city of Logan.  However, this river flooding did not cause much damage. ||Governor Manchin declared a state of emergency.  Around 100 National Guard troops were used in the clean up.  Feeding stations were provided by church and relief organizations.  A few overnight relief shelters were opened.   A presidental disaster declaration was signed for the four counties of Logan, Wyoming, Mcdowell, and Mingo. See FEMA disaster number 1918 for more details.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams that flow into the Mount Gay community from the west and south, such as Mud Fork, Copperas Mine Fork, and Whitman Creek flooded.   This also hit communities such as Verdunville, Cora, Shamrock, and Holden. Further upstream, the streams and mountainsides that drain into the Guyandotte River also flooded.  This included areas such as the south portion fo Man and around Mallory.  Many small hollows were flooded. This would include Madison Branch.  A resident along Madison Branch described the flooding as the mountains letting loose and washing out the ground surrounding the homes of her relatives.  They were unable to get out of the hollow to get water or supplies.  At Taplin, a church was pushed off its foundation and was left sitting on the road.||Preliminary damage accounts for the county, indicated 6 single family homes and 7 mobile homes were destroyed.  Major damage was to 43 single family homes and to 15 mobile homes.  Over a half million dollars in damage to roads and bridges was estimated.

Radar signatures for strong winds were less obvious east and south of Charleston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With plenty of sunshine, a convective complex developed during the heat of the afternoon over central Ohio in a weak wind flow.  The storms intensified as they drifted southeast into southern Ohio and southern West Virginia.  The rain did bring some temporary relief from the heat.  Minor flash flooding occurred.  Rain rates of up to 2 inches in an hour were likely.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few spotters in the city of Huntington measured 3 to 4 inches of rain for the localized maximum rain amounts.  The rain duration was around 3 hours.  Stream and street flooding closed several roads.  Vehicles were flooded and abandoned on the streets.  Fourpole Creek flooded portions of Ritter Park.  The usual viaduct flooding also occurred.  The Cabell County emergency services director said the first responders were overwhelmed and the road closures brought the city to a halt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms moved rapidly east through Kentucky and into West Virginia by the early afternoon.  The movement was toward the east at around 40 mph.  This was a faster flow than observed during the convection on the 4th.  Wind damage was the main hazard.  Some segments bowed out, especially in Putnam County.  Radar signatures for strong winds were less obvious east and south of Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and large branches were blown down or uprooted.  One tree fell onto a house adjacent to the Sleepy Hollow Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed in southeast Ohio during the late morning on the 24th, ahead of a surface cold front.  Winds aloft were stronger than average for late June.  A 30 to 35 mph flow existed at 3 to 10 thousand feet above the ground.  The storms intensified as they moved across northern West Virginia.  Wet microbursts occurred near Weston in Lewis County.  The storms were not quite as strong across southern West Virginia.  Local downpours were common, with rain rates of 1 to 2 inches in less than an hour.  Some training of the cells produced 2 to 3 inches of rain in Upshur County, but flooding was insignificant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple intense downbursts, on the scale of microbursts, occurred just north of downtown Weston.  The damage first occurred along Geelick Road, but increased as the wind descended into the West Fork River Valley in the Smith Village section of Weston.  The included such roads as Cody Lane, Butchers Lane, and Garton Avenue.    The worst damage was in the river valley, after crossing the river, in Deanville.  The damage path fanned out to as wide as 250 yards.  The highest wind gust of around 100 mph was estimated here.  Homes were damaged or destroyed along such streets as Forinash Road and Dovenor Street.  Sheets of the corrugated metal roof to the Deanville stockyard were twisted and ripped off.  Trees were snapped and fell onto power lines.  As a result, the adjacent power poles snapped.  One manufactured home was moved off its foundation, even though it had anchor bolts.   A garage roof was lifted off.  Sections of its cinderblock walls collapsed.  Severe damage occurred to many roofs.   ||Winds from the microbursts also channeled up the hollow along Lynn Avenue.  Further east, scattered tree damage occurred along Smith Run Road. ||Luckily, nobody was injured.  The initial damage assessments had between 2 to 4 homes destroyed.  Major damage occurred to another 5 to 6 homes.||Lewis County was added to the existing FEMA disaster number 1918.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front approached the area in the morning triggering scattered showers and thunderstorms. An outflow boundary from this precipitation area sparked additional thunderstorms in southeast West Virginia, with one producing damaging winds in Greenbrier county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked a tree down near Richlands along Interstate 64.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest wind flow of hot and humid air helped induce scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms. One storm pulsed to a stronger level in Boyd County Kentucky and moved into West Virginia.  Another storm pulsed to a stronger level over the southern mountains of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees fell, some onto power lines in the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well south of a frontal boundary, a moist and unstable air mass resided over the Ohio Valley and Central Appalachians.   The ground was already wetter than normal from previous rains.  A southwest and west wind along with daytime heating formed showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 20th.   Overnight, more storms formed along a leftover east to west boundary.  That allowed for repetitive showers from portions of northeast Kentucky into extreme southern Ohio and western West Virginia.||After some severe thunderstorms during the afternoon, water issues were the main concern overnight.  The main period of rain was from mid afternoon on the 20th into the predawn hours of the 21st.  Rain amounts of 3 to 4 inches were common in parts of Wayne, Cabell, Lincoln, Putnam, and Kanawha Counties.  The city of Wayne measured 3.84 inches of rain in the 24 hours ending at dawn on the 21st, with 1.75 inches falling in 2 hours.   Pea Ridge and Saint Albans both measured around 3.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell onto power lines within the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, in tandem with strong mid and upper level forcing, produced a severe weather outbreak in southeast Ohio into extreme western West Virginia during the late afternoon and evening of Thursday the 16th. The setup featured a 45 knot mid level flow and a 95 knot upper level jet.  Matter of fact, a larger area of showers, associated with a warm front, had moved across southeast Ohio and West Virginia during the morning and midday hours. This kept the surface based instability at a minimum.  However, new cells formed further west during the mid afternoon over western Ohio.  It was this new cluster of convection that would intensify due to the strong mid and upper level dynamics.  Several of the new cells formed and intensified on the southern or southwestern flank of the overall larger area of showers and thunderstorms.  This area of convection stretched further to the northeast into eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania.  This allowed an unimpeded low level moisture inflow into the new development.  Surface dew points ahead of the cold front reached into the mid 60s.||Most of the severe thunderstorms exhibited strong rotation, resulting in the spawning of 5 tornadoes.  Four of these 5 tornadoes were in southeast Ohio.  One of the 4 Ohio tornadoes crossed from Fairfield County into western Perry County.   Another 1 of the 4 crossed from Perry into northwestern Morgan County.  One tornado patch was solely in Athens County.  Finally, the last of the 4 tornadoes in Ohio crossed the Ohio River from northeastern Meigs County into southern Wood County in West Virginia.  This tornado was the strongest and resulted in a fatality on the West Virginia side of the Ohio River.  The last direct death from a tornado in West Virginnia was back in June of 1982.  Finally, the last short tornado path in this outbreak was solely in western West Virginia, located in Wirt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Atlantic Ocean continued to pump in hot and humid air over the Mid-Atlantic. A series of upper-level disturbances in the jet stream passed through eastern West Virginia triggering showers and thunderstorms during this time. There was enough instability from the hot and humid air for some thunderstorms to contain damaging winds during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were down along roads across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage occured along portions of State Route 5 about 3.5 miles eastsoutheast of Palestine.  Numerous hardwoods were topped halfway down their trunks.  Several very large softwoods were either topped or uprooted.  The orientation of the damage conformed to a converging wind flow.  The maximum wind gust of 100 mph was estimated.  The maximum width was around 175 yards.  A brick chimney became detached from a house and collapsed. A few small outbuildings were also destroyed.  Power outages were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed into the area at peak heating of the day creating high instability along with moderate shear. Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of the front as well as more organized lines closer to the front, producing downburst winds but very little hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado originated in northeastern Meigs County Ohio and crossed the Ohio River, just upstream of the lock and dam and the community of Belleville.  A small pocket of EF3 damage located along Route 68 in the valley and flood plain along the river.  The maximum wind gusts were estimated at 160 mph here.  Also, the width of the tornado briefly widened to 500 yards here.  Well built single family homes received major damage or were destroyed.  In this area, a 57 year old male was killed.  He, his wife, and their dog had gone downstairs into the basement for protection.  The husband went back upstair to get a flashlight.  He figured the electricity would go out, as darkness had already set in.  At that point, he disappeared as the tornado hit.  His wife tried to open the door to let him back down, but she could not open the door. Their ranch style home with a brick front was destroyed.  His body was found some 150 to 200 feet away in a field.  Ten other people were injured, but none seriously.  Other significant structural and tree damage occurred along a river access road and basically along the South Fork of Lee Creek drainage toward Rockport.  The total path length of this tornado from Meigs County Ohio into Wood County was over 9 miles.   All total, the county reported 10 homes destroyed, 6 homes with major damage, and 27 homes with minor damage.  About 15 to 18 outbuildings were damaged or destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms associated with a stalled cold front moved over eastern Ohio, southwestern Pennsylvania and the northern West Virginia Panhandle. These thunderstorms produced damaging straight line winds that caused numerous toppled and snapped trees as well as downed power lines that left an estimated 100 thousand people without power across the region. In Ohio, the hardest hit counties were Harrison and Jefferson, where a tractor trailer was overturned on Route 43. Allegheny and Westmoreland counties in western Pennsylvania were also hit hard.

In Ohio, the hardest hit counties were Harrison and Jefferson, where a tractor trailer was overturned on Route 43. Allegheny and Westmoreland counties in western Pennsylvania were also hit hard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newspaper reported that Brooke county 911 received reports of trees down along Eldersville Road near Beechbottom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved along a boundary situated over east central Ohio, southern Pennsylvania and Northern West Virginia. Severe thunderstorms across eastern Ohio, southwest Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland produced widespread wind damage with 50 thousand homes left without power. The counties that were hardest hit were Belmont and Jefferson counties in Ohio, where numerous large trees blocked roadways and three large radio station towers collapsed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported widespread trees down blocking roads throughout Wheeling, with roof damage and a tree down on a house near Mozart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, in tandem with strong mid and upper level forcing, produced a severe weather outbreak in southeast Ohio into extreme western West Virginia during the late afternoon and evening of Thursday the 16th. The setup featured a 45 knot mid level flow and a 95 knot upper level jet.  Matter of fact, a larger area of showers, associated with a warm front, had moved across southeast Ohio and West Virginia during the morning and midday hours. This kept the surface based instability at a minimum.  However, new cells formed further west during the mid afternoon over western Ohio.  It was this new cluster of convection that would intensify due to the strong mid and upper level dynamics.  Several of the new cells formed and intensified on the southern or southwestern flank of the overall larger area of showers and thunderstorms.  This area of convection stretched further to the northeast into eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania.  This allowed an unimpeded low level moisture inflow into the new development.  Surface dew points ahead of the cold front reached into the mid 60s.||Most of the severe thunderstorms exhibited strong rotation, resulting in the spawning of 5 tornadoes.  Four of these 5 tornadoes were in southeast Ohio.  One of the 4 Ohio tornadoes crossed from Fairfield County into western Perry County.   Another 1 of the 4 crossed from Perry into northwestern Morgan County.  One tornado patch was solely in Athens County.  Finally, the last of the 4 tornadoes in Ohio crossed the Ohio River from northeastern Meigs County into southern Wood County in West Virginia.  This tornado was the strongest and resulted in a fatality on the West Virginia side of the Ohio River.  The last direct death from a tornado in West Virginnia was back in June of 1982.  Finally, the last short tornado path in this outbreak was solely in western West Virginia, located in Wirt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Road Run.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms, some severe, dropped southeast from the Ohio Valley late on the afternoon of the 4th producing several reports of wind damage across parts of southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were reported down on the west side of Rupert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed ahead of a strong autumn cold front.  The front was associated with the record setting low pressure system lifting northeast through Minnesota into Canada.  The front passed through West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  Strong to severe wind gusts occurred near the squall line and in its wake.  The leading squall line began to weaken as it moved further east into West Virginia.  Temperatures dropped rapidly from around 80 degrees to near 60 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

A quarter to a half inch of ice accumulated.  However, no widespread electrical outages occurred.  Elsewhere, the precipitation stayed mainly snow, where 4 to 6 inches accumulated.

A squall line developed ahead of a strong autumn cold front.  The front was associated with the record setting low pressure system lifting northeast through Minnesota into Canada.  The front passed through West Virginia during the late afternoon and early evening.  Strong to severe wind gusts occurred near the squall line and in its wake.  The leading squall line began to weaken as it moved further east into West Virginia.  Temperatures dropped rapidly from around 80 degrees to near 60 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From the late afternoon on November 30th to the early morning of December 1st, rain amounts of 2 to 3 inches fell over the headwaters of the Elk and Gauley Rivers in Webster, Nicholas, western Pocahontas, and northwestern Greenbrier Counties.   Luckily, the ground was relatively dry prior to this event.  Minor flooding still occurred along the headwater streams and rivers in Webster and Nicholas Counties during the predawn to sunrise time frame on the 1st. ||No structures were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Fork of the Cherry River flooded yards and surrounded one home along Johnstown Road near Richwood.  The staff gauge in Richwood, located near the junction of the North and South Forks of the Cherry River, crested around 9 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strenthening low pressure system moved northeast from Kentucky into northern Ohio on the 16th.  A brief squall line developed well ahead of the associated cold front.  Wind gusts of 40 to around 50 mph were felt in portions of West Virginia.
Scattered thunderstorms with very heavy rain produced flash flooding across northern West Virginia and western Pennsylvania ahead of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported widespread flash flooding in and around Morgantown with numerous road closures. Up to 4 feet of water was flowing across roads in some locations with 2 feet of water reported in an equipment room on the campus of West Virginia University. A woman was rescued from her vehicle on the White Ave. bridge when her car became stranded by the flood waters of Deckers Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With plenty of sunshine, a convective complex developed during the heat of the afternoon over central Ohio in a weak wind flow.  The storms intensified as they drifted southeast into southern Ohio and southern West Virginia.  The rain did bring some temporary relief from the heat.  Minor flash flooding occurred.  Rain rates of up to 2 inches in an hour were likely.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams flooded and closed roads around Glen Rogers.  These streams were coming off of Guyandotte Mountain and feeding the headwaters of Laurel Fork.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon over western Ohio and southeast Indiana.  This was well south of a front, but along a boundary leftover from previous convection.  The storms intensified as they moved into northeast Kentucky and southwest West Virginia during the evening hours.  Wind damage was the main hazard.  However, brief localized downpours of an inch in 30 minutes did cause the usual minor street flooding.  The storms weakened during the late evening hours as they moved further east in West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down in the Kenova vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms moved rapidly east through Kentucky and into West Virginia by the early afternoon.  The movement was toward the east at around 40 mph.  This was a faster flow than observed during the convection on the 4th.  Wind damage was the main hazard.  Some segments bowed out, especially in Putnam County.  Radar signatures for strong winds were less obvious east and south of Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Hog Knob Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst blew down 80 to 100 trees at the Sleepy Hollow Golf Course.  Tree damage also extended into the adjacent Woods and Irons subdivision.  Damage to dwellings was minor.  Wind damage had occurred in this same area just over a week earlier, on the 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms with very heavy rain produced flash flooding across northern West Virginia and western Pennsylvania ahead of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported flash flooding in Moundsville with streets closed due to flowing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level disturbance and its associated cold front moved into West Virginia on the 25th. Hot and humid conditions ahead of the front led to moderate amounts of instability. Showers and thunderstorms developed and some thunderstorms became severe due to the instability and strong winds aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and limbs were down along Route 259 and Mild Road in Yellow Spring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a hot and humid air, thunderstorms moved through southeast Ohio and into West Virginia during the afternoon.  Low level convergence was aided by a leftover boundary from early morning convection.  The thunderstorms moved southeast around 25 to 30 mph with wind gusts the main hazard.  The tall thunderstorms also produced considerable cloud to ground lightning.  Power outages were common.  One fatality occurred in Kanawha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the area from the north which triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms. A couple of these storms produced severe weather in southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds broke the top of a tree and blew down several large branches. Corn plants were also flattened by the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the area from the north which triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms. A couple of these storms produced severe weather in southeast West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped several 4 to 6 inch diameter tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a hot and humid air, thunderstorms moved through southeast Ohio and into West Virginia during the afternoon.  Low level convergence was aided by a leftover boundary from early morning convection.  The thunderstorms moved southeast around 25 to 30 mph with wind gusts the main hazard.  The tall thunderstorms also produced considerable cloud to ground lightning.  Power outages were common.  One fatality occurred in Kanawha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and large branches were blown down from the East End of Charleston on toward Kanawha State Forest.  Some branches fell onto vehicles.  The dark screen at  the baseball field was shredded by the wind. ||A delayed direct death occurred in a freak accident about 2 hours after the storms had ended.  A man, his 2 children, and his mom were traveling in a 4 door sedan near Kanawha State Forest just before 1900E.  They had tried to attend a midweek church service, but the service was canceled due to a power outage.  A large tree, weakened by the storm, fell off a hillside and directly onto their moving vehicle.  The roof was smashed.  The 62 year old mother was killed.  She was the passenger in the front seat.  The driver was seriously injured.  The 2 children were not seriously hurt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north on the 12th, opening the door to abundant moisture.  A cluster of thunderstorms trained along the Ohio River during the night.  Stream and street flooding occurred around Saint Marys of Pleasants County.||Additional rounds of showers and thunderstorms formed during the day on the 13th, causing minor street and stream flooding.  However, some wind damage did occur in Kanawha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Limestone Run flooded and closed County Route 12.  No dwelling were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a hot and humid air, thunderstorms moved through southeast Ohio and into West Virginia during the afternoon.  Low level convergence was aided by a leftover boundary from early morning convection.  The thunderstorms moved southeast around 25 to 30 mph with wind gusts the main hazard.  The tall thunderstorms also produced considerable cloud to ground lightning.  Power outages were common.  One fatality occurred in Kanawha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large branches fell onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of showers and thunderstorms moved rapidly east through Kentucky and into West Virginia by the early afternoon.  The movement was toward the east at around 40 mph.  This was a faster flow than observed during the convection on the 4th.  Wind damage was the main hazard.  Some segments bowed out, especially in Putnam County.  Radar signatures for strong winds were less obvious east and south of Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Oak Forest Road.  A house was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air ahead of a cold front in the Ohio Valley combined with an upper level low pressure system to spark isolated severe thunderstorms across Greenbrier county, West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were knocked down by thunderstorm winds on Cold Knob.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air ahead of a cold front in the Ohio Valley combined with an upper level low pressure system to spark isolated severe thunderstorms across Greenbrier county, West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused a tree to split along Highway 219.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With plenty of sunshine, a convective complex developed during the heat of the afternoon over central Ohio in a weak wind flow.  The storms intensified as they drifted southeast into southern Ohio and southern West Virginia.  The rain did bring some temporary relief from the heat.  Minor flash flooding occurred.  Rain rates of up to 2 inches in an hour were likely.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streams flooded roads and caused debris slides.  Water flooded a few parked cars near Helen.  An automatic rain gauge on Tams Mountain measured 2.94 inches in less than 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and an embedded thunderstorm associated with a cold front moving across the region produced isolated wind damage in Preston County, West Virginia early in the morning on Spetember 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager in Preston County reported trees down in Kingwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly flow, ahead of a cold front, transported very moist air through Tennessee and Kentucky.  Surface dew points were in the mid and upper 60s.  Wave after wave in the mid and upper levels helped trigger widespread showers, with embedded thunderstorms.  These training rains entered northeast Kentucky and southeast Ohio during the morning of May 2nd.  However, the rain was slow to move east, not becoming steady until late afternoon for portions of western West Virginia. The heaviest rains fell during the evening hours to after midnight.  However, light to moderate rain lingered until the predawn hours of Monday the 3rd.||The rain was not as heavy in West Virginia, when compared to counties to the west, in northeast Kentucky and southeast Ohio.  The heaviest rain rates in West Virginia were on the order of .5 to 1 inch in less than 3 hours.||Rain totals of 2.25 to 3.0 inches were widespread along the Ohio River counties, with a few locations in Cabell and northwestern Mason County exceeding 3.5 inches.  The 24 hours rain amount at the cooperative observer in Huntington was 3.63 inches.  However, further east in Cabell County, rain amounts were less.  Pea Ridge measured 2.2 inches of rain. The Tri State Airport in northern Wayne County had 3.1 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams flooded roads.  One example was Jerico Road or County Route 16 being closed by the flood waters from Crooked Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strenthening low pressure system moved northeast from Kentucky into northern Ohio on the 16th.  A brief squall line developed well ahead of the associated cold front.  Wind gusts of 40 to around 50 mph were felt in portions of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 51 mph was measured.  This caused a few tree branches to be blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Appalachian Mountains early on the morning of December 1 into an unseasonably warm and moisture atmosphere in the Mid-Atlantic. Widespread rain and thunderstorms developed, some of which produced heavy rain and flash flooding across the area. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to over 2 inches across most locations, with the highest report received in Berkeley County of 2.57 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High waterwas reported on Patterson Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a hot and humid air, thunderstorms moved through southeast Ohio and into West Virginia during the afternoon.  Low level convergence was aided by a leftover boundary from early morning convection.  The thunderstorms moved southeast around 25 to 30 mph with wind gusts the main hazard.  The tall thunderstorms also produced considerable cloud to ground lightning.  Power outages were common.  One fatality occurred in Kanawha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and large branches damaged power lines and a transformer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a hot and humid air, thunderstorms moved through southeast Ohio and into West Virginia during the afternoon.  Low level convergence was aided by a leftover boundary from early morning convection.  The thunderstorms moved southeast around 25 to 30 mph with wind gusts the main hazard.  The tall thunderstorms also produced considerable cloud to ground lightning.  Power outages were common.  One fatality occurred in Kanawha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in town and along Parkersburg Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well south of a frontal boundary, a moist and unstable air mass resided over the Ohio Valley and Central Appalachians.   The ground was already wetter than normal from previous rains.  A southwest and west wind along with daytime heating formed showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 20th.   Overnight, more storms formed along a leftover east to west boundary.  That allowed for repetitive showers from portions of northeast Kentucky into extreme southern Ohio and western West Virginia.||After some severe thunderstorms during the afternoon, water issues were the main concern overnight.  The main period of rain was from mid afternoon on the 20th into the predawn hours of the 21st.  Rain amounts of 3 to 4 inches were common in parts of Wayne, Cabell, Lincoln, Putnam, and Kanawha Counties.  The city of Wayne measured 3.84 inches of rain in the 24 hours ending at dawn on the 21st, with 1.75 inches falling in 2 hours.   Pea Ridge and Saint Albans both measured around 3.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Left Fork of the Mud River flooded Bulger Road and the local fire station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the region on the 24th. Warm and humid air ahead of the cold front caused plenty of instability. The instability combined with the forcing from the front to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Some thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down near Circleville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short lines and clusters of thunderstorms formed ahead of a cold front.   Large hail occurred from the strongest storms during the late afternoon.

A line of thunderstorms formed over eastern Indiana during the mid afternoon.  The thunderstorms intensified in the afternoon heat, as they moved east across southern Ohio.  The leading edge of the storms moved across the Ohio River and into western West Virginnia after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through the region late during the evening of the 16th into the early morning morning hours of the 17th. A strong southerly flow ahead of the cold front transported plenty of moisture into the area allowing for limited instability to develop. Strong forcing associated with the frontal passage combined with limited instability to trigger showers and thunderstorms. Thunderstorms were able to mix down very strong winds aloft causing damaging winds across some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down near Winebrenners Crossroad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short lines and clusters of thunderstorms formed ahead of a cold front.   Large hail occurred from the strongest storms during the late afternoon.

Scattered severe thunderstorms developed across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland ahead of a cold front moving east across the Ohio Valley. One long lived supercell produced large hail across northern West Virginia and Garrett county, Maryland.

As a cold front moved southeast across the upper Ohio Valley, scattered severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. A supercell thunderstorm produced an EF1 tornado in Carroll and Jefferson counties in Ohio, with numerous reports of wind damage and hail up to golf ball size elsewhere. Flooding of streams and creeks also occurred over western Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and the northern panhandle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down in New Cumberland.
